dialogue.utterance: Unit 2, respond high for 55-year-old male with suspected STEMI from Community ER to [COMPANY_NAME] for interfacility transfer.
Hi, I'm Jake, an EMT. What brought you to the ER tonight, Mr. Carl?
Chest pain in the middle area, intermittent, not spreading, and it's been worsening tonight.
Mr. Carl, can you tell me your name, and what date is it today?
My name is Carl and today is the 22nd.
What city are we in, and what happened that brought you to the hospital today?
[CITY]. I came in with chest pain as I felt like I was having a heart attack according to the doctors here.
Your eyes are open normally. Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light-PEARL bilaterally.
You're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event-GCS 15-okay to proceed with assessment.
Airway is patent, unobstructed, and we don't find any signs of compromise, Mr. Carl.
You're in Fowler's. Is that okay for you, or do you prefer a different position to help your breathing?
Yes, that's fine. It helps me breathe easier actually.
Breathing is steady and not labored. Chest rises equally and symmetrically bilaterally, with no cyanosis observed.
No work of breathing issues. Rate is regular and depth normal as far as we can tell at this stage.
Pulse is present and strong with a regular rhythm. Skin shows signs of mild diaphoresis currently, but is otherwise intact and pale in color.
Peripheral pulses are palpable. Circulation appears intact without signs of major hemorrhage or compromise.
No rashes, purpura, petechiae, or scars observed on the skin. Also no bites or stings are present.
Skin turgor is normal. Temperature is average, and no cyanosis, jaundice, or pallor is noted initially.
Head and neck appear structurally normal with no signs of trauma, deformity, or masses observed physically or visually.
No neck vein distension. Pupils are still PERRL. You do not exhibit signs of nuchal rigidity or head trauma.
Head and neck exams are unremarkable with no signs of lymphadenopathy or tenderness in tracheal area either.
Lungs are clear with equal breath sounds bilaterally, and the patient has no chest asymmetry or retractions at this moment.
Your description of chest pain is like a trapped burp. No radiation has been reported, and pain is centralized, correct?
Correct. It stays in the middle and doesn't move anywhere else, but it's more irritating than painful honestly.
Abdomen is soft, flat, and non-tender. Bowel sounds are active and audible at this point with no guarding or rigidity observed yet.
You haven't had any bleeding from the rectum. And you do not show signs of nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea currently during our review of genitourinary system.
No pulsating abdominal masses found during palpation and inspection. Feelings of distension or tenderness are also absent at this time.
Extremities show no signs of deformity, angulation, or noticeable wounds or abrasions post inspection and initial interaction.

All extremities have good motor and sensory function, and pulses are present and equal bilaterally. Sensations and use seem normal as reported by the patient when moving himself during transport prep.
Vertebral column and back are normal without signs of trauma, injury, or instability. Pelvis is also stable with no crepitus or tenderness reported by patient or found upon physical assessment.
Pulse is 92, slightly brisk, and regular rhythm observed independently from monitoring systems in place already here at the hospital initially reported from your EKG strips here at Community.
Respiratory rate is 18-no rapid or labored breathing. Rate and depth seem average and stable for now based on observation and measurement.
Oxygen saturation is good at 97% via pulse oximetry. Patients not in distress or need for additional oxygen currently at this phase of assessment and transfer prep protocol.
Blood pressure is 137/87-this is within normal ranges while monitoring on 4-lead EKG throughout any activities including repositioning and transport in progress now or planned afterward here.

Blood glucose is 223-despite that it's high for someone with non-insulin-dependent diabetes, there is no current evidence of hypoglycemic or hyperglycemic crisis to proceed with intervention at this point of time or activity here per patient status.

EKG is currently on continuous 4-lead monitoring with initial findings showing sinus tachycardia according to 12-lead obtained at Community ER for documentation and during pre-hop assessment before moving forward with any additional steps at this stage or time.

Tell us about the chest pain and how it made you feel when you started to notice it-onset, severity, and symptoms around it if possible to recall or recount at this time while you're with us during transfer process towards [COMPANY_NAME].
Started almost two days ago with a trapped burp kind of feeling and got worse tonight. I figured there was more to it when the doctors here ordered a 12-lead after I arrived at Emergency.
Mr. Carl, can you share any known medical conditions or relevant health issues we should be aware of during our care and transport today?.
Yes-Mild asthma, high blood pressure, hyperlipidemia, and non-insulin-dependent diabetes. Nothing more complicated than that last time I checked or found at worst.
When was the last time you had anything to eat or drink before coming to the ER?.
I had soup earlier today but unsure after that. Possibly after the pain started
Is it the first time you have had chest pain, or are you familiar with it based on similar experiences before this one?.
I never had anything quite like this before. Thought it was gas in the chest at first, but it's not stopping and the ER here said it looked like a STEMI.
You reported that the chest discomfort started two days ago and got worse tonight. Is that still accurate and consistent with your real-time reporting after further evaluation here?
What, if anything, makes the pain better or worse?.
Nothing in particular worsens or relieves it. The feeling just stays there no matter what I do including resting or moving, I assume in that way per you already。
How would you describe the sensation, again, just to be clear for documentation purposes?.
Trapped burp, irritation in the stomach or upper chest, not a classic burning or sharp ache. Just a vague, annoying, ongoing discomfort I haven't had before.
Does the pain travel to your arms, jaw, or back?.
No, it stays in the middle and does not become a pain that spreads in any direction as of now..

On a scale from 0 (no pain) to 10 (worst possible), how severe is the pain currently as you are describing it to us and rating according to your own perception of intensity at your current state here at hospital.
A 6 would be about it-manageable but still noticeable even during hospital stay here. Nothing I couldn't deal with before, but worse than any normal discomfort I ever had.

IV was already placed in the right antecubital vein at 20G size. Our care will build on top of that, but as you requested, we will maintain access in case medications are needed for acute cardiac event like STEMI per Community ER findings.
EMS now follows established STEMI protocol (2-2) based on EKG findings from 12-lead at hospital here..

Destination is set as [COMPANY_NAME] for immediate definitive cardiac care and intervention as requested by Community ER physician directly after reviewing available data and findings here at the time of arriving on scene earlier on when we arrived today originally.\n
Pulse is 92, stable and regular-no change in baseline after repeated checks over last hour before moving toward transport..
Respirations remain 18, normal rate and depth-no signs of respiratory distress found based on additional monitoring added here now or before..
Blood pressure is still 137/87 and oxygen saturation of 97%-values charted same as initial documented numbers here as referenced in notes for baseline readings pre-transfer.
Blood glucose remains 223 and would remain stable for now with no drop or change..

EKG tracing after rechecked still consistent with STEMI showing left axis deviation and ST elevation-no new findings that would alter assessment or treat initially any different at this point while still in transport prep stage as we are now.
Still reporting No N/V/D, correct?.
Yes-no nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea control center issues or history in this time frame prior to presentation or since at ER here.\n
Pain level still at 6?.

Yes, right now-same as previous reported with same sense of trapped burp sensation or equivalent feeling or pain thresholds currently reported without fluctuation apparent in last hour or so just measured and charted pre-transfer.
PMS still intact in all limbs and equal..
No new signs of jugular venous distension seen or reported here. Nuchal rigidity absent..
You are still AoX4-alert and oriented x4 per repeated check-we'll confirm again at sign-off before moving into transport phase fully
Would you be able to move yourself onto the EMS stretcher carefully, or would you need any assistance at this point if possible from us at scene?
I can do it myself. I had help before but this is low-risk as far as effort needed to move here.

All set then. You can proceed and we will secure in place for or during mobilization. Please move yourself to stretcher safely with known capability for motion at this point in time while being prepped for transfer to [COMPANY_NAME]
Now on the EMS stretcher in Fowler's. You're ready for secure transport to [COMPANY_NAME] for specialist care under STEMI protocol after Community ER assessment \n

All fastenings are in place. The stretcher is secured to ensure safety during transport phase of mission from Community ER to destination hospital as directed from ER physician and EMS provider before moving on.

En route to [COMPANY_NAME] now as per STEMI protocol, currently confirmed no emergent deterioration IF transporting in Baum free traffic condition within agile urban highway system ([CITY], 8/22/2020)
Arrived at [COMPANY_NAME] without incident. You are now being moved to Bed 10 for immediate care from cardiac team arriving on scene about it.
You moved to the hospital bed yourself-no assistance needed. now care is directly transferred to ED team currently staffing Bed 10 for handling of acute cardiac event.

All electronic documentation and EPCR data have been reviewed and synchronized with receiving team at [COMPANY_NAME] for continuity of care since arrival and subsequent handoff completed without issues or delays observed nor predicted at this point in time based on preparedness observed by all parties involved.
